content:
# Imaging and Deconvolution

Joshua Marvil, NRAO

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop

28 May 2026

# Key Concepts

Visibility – a measurement of one Fourier component of the sky brightness distribution.

Gridding – combine all the measured visibilities onto a UV grid

Imaging – take the inverse Fourier transform of the gridded visibilities

Point spread function – a convolutional image feature due to incomplete UV sampling; the ‘dirty beam’

Deconvolution – the removal of the PSF from the image; ‘cleaning’

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
SSS

# Visibility $\leftrightarrow$ Sky relationship

An interferometer directly measures components of the Fourier transform of the intensity distribution

After several simplifying assumptions, we can relate our measurements to the sky brightness:

$$
V _ {\nu} (u, v) = \iint I _ {\nu} (l, m) e ^ {- 2 \pi i (u l + v m)} d l d m
$$

which can be directly inverted as:

$$
I _ {\nu} (l, m) = \iint V _ {\nu} (u, v) e ^ {2 \pi i (u l + v m)} d u d v
$$

(adapted from from Taylor, Carilli, & Perley)

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
3

# Visibility ↔ Sky relationship

Transmit-receive reciprocity in antenna theory:
a system can be treated either as a receiver or a transmitter
and it will have the same directional gain pattern

receiver

transmitter

# Visibility $\leftrightarrow$ Sky relationship

If $f(x)$ is real, $F(s)$ is Hermitian: $f(x) = f^{k}(-x)$

The sky is Real, so our Visibilities are Hermitian:

$\rightarrow$ conjugate visibilities

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
6

# Visibility $\leftrightarrow$ Sky relationship

**Earth rotation synthesis**: a baseline and its conjugate trace out elliptical arcs through the u-v plane over time, based on its Earth-centric coordinates $X_{\lambda}, Y_{\lambda}, Z_{\lambda}$, the source's declination $\delta$, and the range of hour angles $H$ observed

semi-major axis $\sqrt{X_{\lambda}^{2} + Y_{\lambda}^{2}}$

semi-minor axis $\sin \delta_0\sqrt{X_\lambda^2 + Y_\lambda^2}$

displacement along $\pmb{v}$ $Z_{\lambda}\cos \delta_{0}$

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{l} u \\ v \\ w \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc} \sin H & \cos H & 0 \\ - \sin \delta \cos H & \sin \delta \sin H & \cos \delta \\ \cos \delta \cos H & - \cos \delta \sin H & \sin \delta \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{l} X _ {\lambda} \\ Y _ {\lambda} \\ Z _ {\lambda} \end{array} \right]
$$

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
7

# Table of visibilities

## Essential columns:
- $\mathbf{b}$ in wavelengths or meters
- calibrated $\mathbf{V}$ in Janskys = $10^{-26} \, \mathrm{Wm}^{-2} \, \mathrm{Hz}^{-1}$
- frequency, phase-tracking center

## Other columns:
- data weight, flags
- timestamps, intervals
- antenna & receiver IDs

many, many rows

|  b (u, v, w) | V (x+iy) | direction | frequency | ...  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |   |   |   |   |
|  |   |   |   |   |
|  |   |   |   |   |
|  |   |   |   |   |

Ancillary tables: antenna pointing, weather, system calibration

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
12

# Imaging with the Fourier transform

Performance considerations for M visibilities, NxN image size:

- Direct Fourier Transform:  $O(M^{*}N^{2})$
- Discrete Fourier Transform:  $O(N^4)$
- Fast Fourier Transform:  $O(N^2 \log_2 N)$

require regular grid

Typical sizes:  $M \sim 10^{6-10}$ ;  $N \sim 10^{2-4+}$

Typical runtimes: Discrete  $\sim$  days; FFT  $\sim$  minutes

JYF 2023

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging

# Gridding

Practical to think first about the image grid:

- predict your angular resolution from diffraction:  $\theta_{\mathrm{syn}} \approx \frac{\lambda}{b_{\mathrm{max}}}$  (radians)
- choose an image cell size  $\Delta l$  that oversamples  $\theta_{\mathrm{syn}}$  by a factor of  $4\sim 5$
- choose an image size MxN (pixels) based on your desired field of view (FOV)
- FOV will be  $M\Delta l \times N\Delta m$
- typically want  $\Delta l = \Delta m, M = N$

A

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging

# Gridding

UV grid properties will be determined from the image grid

- the number of UV grid cells (pixels) will be the same as what was chosen for the image size, MxN
- the UV cell size will be inversely proportional to the image FOV:

$$
\Delta u = 1 / (M \Delta l)
$$

$$
\Delta v = 1 / (N \Delta m)
$$

A

#

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging

# Gridding

Visibilities are averaged in the correlator and written out as discrete samples

The correlator integration times are often 1 - 10 seconds

Measured visibilities will have values of u and v that do not align with grid cells, but the FFT requires a grid with regular spacing

SVESEAS 180

A

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
16

# Gridding

The most basic gridding process is called **nearest neighbor**

Move each visibility to the center of the closest UV cell

If there is more than one visibility per cell then take the weighted average, using the **data weights** → **cell averaging**

Keep track of the sum of weights in each UV cell using a separate grid → **gridded weights**

|  |   |   |   |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|   | 1 | 2 |  |   |
|   |  | 1 | 2 |   |
|   |  |  | 1 |   |
|  |   |   |   |   |

SVESEAS 180

A

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging

# Gridding

We can improve the gridding process through **convolutional resampling**

Each visibility is convolved by a **gridding kernel** that distributes the visibility across multiple cells

We can oversample the kernel to reduce interpolation errors

Specific kernels can address other issues including:
- aliasing
- w-term
- mosaicking

The effect of the kernel can be divided out in the image plane

S&S 350

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
18

# First Image of the Visibilities

The following equation requires that we know $V(u, v)$ at all values of $u, v$

$$
I _ {\nu} (l, m) = \iint V _ {\nu} (u, v) e ^ {2 \pi i (u l + v m)} d u d v
$$

but we know that our UV coverage is incomplete. So we add a new sampling function term $S(u, v)$

$$
I _ {\nu} ^ {D} (l, m) = \iint V _ {\nu} (u, v) S (u, v) e ^ {2 \pi i (u l + v m)} d u d v
$$

where

$$
S (u, v) = \sum_ {k = 1} ^ {M} \delta (u - u _ {k}, v - v _ {k}).
$$

M: # of visibility measurements

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
SIX SYNTHESIS

# First Image of the Visibilities

from the multiplication property of the Fourier transform, we can equate

$$
I _ {\nu} ^ {D} (l, m) = \iint V _ {\nu} (u, v) S (u, v) e ^ {2 \pi i (u l + v m)} d u d v
$$

with

$$
\begin{array}{l} I _ {\nu} ^ {D} (l, m) = \mathcal{F} ^ {- 1} [ V _ {\nu} (u, v) ] * \mathcal{F} ^ {- 1} [ S _ {\nu} (u, v) ] \\ = I _ {\nu} (l, m) * B _ {\nu} (l, m) \\ \end{array}
$$

we call  $I^{D}(l,m)$  the dirty image and  $B(l,m)$  the dirty beam

Reversing this convolution to recover  $I(l, m)$  is called deconvolution

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
20

# First Image of the Visibilities

Example dirty image resulting from the convolution of the sky model with the dirty beam. The dirty beam is also called the point spread function.

$$
I _ {\nu} ^ {D} (l, m) = I _ {\nu} (l, m) * B (l, m)
$$

(Figure by U. Rao)

SUNYAKAMAR

#

AIPS

21st

21

Synthesis Imaging Workshop - Imaging

# The Point Spread Function (PSF)

The PSF is computed from the iFFT of the **gridded weights**. We define the ‘**clean beam**’ as a Gaussian fit to the central peak of the PSF.

Properties of the PSF can be adjusted through manipulation of these weights, at the expense of image sensitivity:

- **natural weighting**: no adjustments; optimal averaging
- **uniform weighting**: constant weight per cell
- **super-uniform weighting**: constant weight density
- **robust (Briggs) weighting**: in between uniform and natural
- **(outer) UV taper**: weight decreases with increasing UV-distance

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
22

# The Point Spread Function (PSF)

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
23

|  Natural | Robust 0.7 | Uniform | Tapered Uniform  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  Bm : 5.6 arcsec 0.1 sidelobe | Bm : 4.0 arcsec 0.05 sidelobe | Bm : 3.2 arcsec +0.03,-0.08 sidelobe | Bm : 8.0arcsec 0.01 sidelobe  |
|  |   |   |   |
|  |   |   |   |

# Deconvolution

There is a formal, linear solution to the general problem of deconvolution, called the principal solution

$$
I ^ {sky} (l, m) = F ^ {- 1} \left[ \frac{F [ I ^ {obs} (l , m) ]}{F [ I ^ {PSF} (l , m) ]} \right]
$$

The principal solution is equivalent to the uniformly weighted dirty image

Linear deconvolution can not fill in the unmeasured UV cells

The FT of these zero-weight cells treats them as having zero value

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
24

# Deconvolution

The FT of zero-weight cells treats them as having zero value, leading to spatial filtering

UV max due to the longest baseline
→ resolution limit, low-pass filter

UV min due to $b_{\min} = D_{\text{antenna}}$
→ largest angular scale (LAS)
→ high-pass filter

Missing data in between samples: spatial frequencies that are missing from the dirty image
→ PSF structure

(Figure adapted from Thompson, Moran, & Swenson)

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
25

# Deconvolution

Deconvolution results can be improved with non-linear methods, e.g.

- Maximum Entropy
- Non-negative Least Squares
- Bayesian Inference
- CLEAN

These methods work by creating a model that is a good fit to the sampled visibilities. The FT of these models is not required to be zero at the location of unmeasured cells.

The final image is created from the *model image* without using any FTs. Therefore, only the residuals (data-model), which are hopefully noise-like with no remaining signal, are convolved by the dirty beam.

S&T SYSTEMS

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop - Imaging

# Deconvolution

The most basic CLEAN algorithm has the following phases:

Use the iFT to create the PSF and dirty image

Run the following loop until some stopping threshold is met:

- Find the brightest peak P in the dirty image
- Measure the position of the peak $(x_p, y_p)$
- Shift the PSF to $(x_p, y_p)$, multiply the PSF by loop gain $\pmb{\nu}$ times P, and subtract this from the dirty image
- Add a point source with flux $\pmb{\nu}\mathbf{P}$ to the model at $(x_p, y_p)$

Smooth the model by a Gaussian clean beam having size $\sim$ PSF

Add the residual image to the smoothed model

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
28

# Deconvolution

Simulated example from the Australian Telescope Compact Array

Model: 5 point sources and 1 Gaussian

Clean iterations:

1,5,10,20,50,100,200,500, 1000

Dirty image

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
29

# Deconvolution

Variations of the CLEAN algorithm:

Major cycles: gridding, FTs, UV-subtraction
- Hogbom: only one cycle, large PSF
- Clark: many cycles, using the gridded data, PSF patch
- Cotton-Schwab: many cycles, using the measured data, PSF patch

Minor cycles: PSF subtraction, model building
- Hogbom: only point sources
- Multi-scale: point and a set of fixed paraboloids
- Adaptive Scale Pixel (ASP): point and a set of variable Gaussians

constrain the location of model components with masks

SUN-SEANN UNIVERSITY
A

# Deconvolution

S&E Media, Inc.
AGI

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
33

# Summary

Visibility – a measurement of one Fourier component of the sky brightness distribution, made in the $u, v$ space

Dirty image – the inverse transform of the measured visibilities. Typically created by evaluating the visibilities on a regular grid, then using the Fast Fourier Transform.

Point spread function – what every point source looks like in the dirty image; also called the dirty beam. It is the FT of the gridded weights.

Deconvolution – replaces occurrences of the dirty beam in the dirty image by a Gaussian of equivalent size to make a ‘cleaned’ image; this works by constructing a sky model in the image domain.

21st Synthesis Imaging Workshop – Imaging
34
